coredata.dc:title: Enhanced selectivity of microfluidic gas sensors by modifying microchannel geometry and surface chemistry with graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ghazi, Mahan
Janfaza, Sajjad
Tahmooressi, Hamed
Ravishankara, Adithya
Earl, Emily
Tasnim, Nishat
Hoorfar, Mina
coredata.dc:description: Among the gas sensing technologies, microfluidic gas sensors have garnered attention because of their sensitivity, compact size, and low cost. In this study, we demonstrate improved selectivity of microfluidic gas sensors toward volatile organic compounds by increasing the effect of adsorption of analytes on the surface of the sensor’s microchannel through increasing the ratio of the surface area (in contact with analyte) to the volume of the microchannel. First, the effect of microchannel geometry modification (reduction of width) is studied through a computational parametric approach (which is validated experimentally). The results show an average improvement of 93.44 % and 60.1 % in selectivity toward polar and nonpolar VOCs, respectively. In the next step, the surface of the microchannel is modified with graphene quantum dots, which has a two-fold effect on VOCs adsorption: (i) increasing the surface area, and (ii) adding functional groups. The experimental results of this step show an average improvement of 101.45 % and 98.82 % in the sensor’s selectivity for the smallest widths toward polar and nonpolar VOCs, respectively. These results indicate that increasing the ratio of surface area (in contact with analyte) to the volume of the microchannel and adding functionalized nanofeatures to the microchannel surface area are promising ways to enhance the selectivity of microfluidic gas sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microfluidic gas sensors
Graphene quantum dots
Selectivity
Nanomaterials
Microfabrication

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of decreasing width of the microchannel
Effect of combination of both modifications

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by MITACS, Inc./Accelerate with FortisBC Energy Inc. Grant number F19-05968 and NSERCDGDND-2018-05726.
This work was supported by MITACS, Inc./Accelerate with FortisBC Energy Inc. Grant number F19-05968 and NSERC DGDND-2018-05726 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) The schematic of the simulation model consisted of the inlet/outlet, microchannel, and MOS sensor housing. b) Image of a typical microfluidic gas sensor consisted of the inlet/outlet, microchannel, and MOS sensor. c) Schematic of the experimental setup.

Comparison of the computational and experimental sensor responses upon exposure to a) 100 ppm of methanol, and b) 100 ppm of ethanol (300 ℃ at the sensing layer of the MOS sensor). c) Result of the mesh independency analysis.

a) Resistances of the unmodified microfluidic gas sensor exposed to 100 ppm of methanol, ethanol, propanol, pentanol, hexane, hexanal, and toluene. b) Responses and response times of unmodified microfluidic gas sensor exposed to 100 ppm of seven different analytes. c) 2D feature representation of the unmodified microfluidic gas sensor exposed to100 ppm of seven different analytes (300 ℃ at the sensing layer of the MOS sensor).

a) Resistances of the 0.5-mm width microfluidic gas sensor exposed to 100 ppm of methanol, ethanol, propanol, pentanol, hexane, hexanal, and toluene. b) Responses and response times of microfluidic gas sensor (with the width of 0.5 mm) exposed to seven different VOCs. c) 2D feature representation of the 0.5-mm width microfluidic gas sensor exposed to seven different analytes (300 ℃ at the sensing layer of the MOS sensor).

Guiding graphs to choose the optimal width based on the application needs. a) Average selectivity versus average sensor response (
Ra
Rg
), and b) average selectivity versus average response time.

a) Emission spectra of the GQDs (200- 800 nm), b) FTIR spectra of the GQDs. Contact angles of microchannels c) before and d) after modification with GQDs. SEM images of microchannel e) before and after coating with f) Parylene C, and g) GQDs.

a)Resistances of GQDs-coated microfluidic gas sensor exposed to methanol, ethanol, propanol, pentanol, hexane, hexanal, and toluene. b) Responses and response times of the GQDs-coated microfluidic gas sensor exposed to 100 ppm of seven different analytes. c) 2D feature representation of GQDs-coated microfluidic gas sensor exposed to 100 ppm of seven different analytes (300 ℃ at the sensing layer of the MOS sensor).

a) Resistances of the 0.5-mm width and GQDs-coated microfluidic gas sensors exposed to 100 ppm of methanol, ethanol, propanol, pentanol, hexane, hexanal, and toluene. b) Responses and response times of 0.5-mm width GQDs-coated microfluidic gas sensor exposed to 100 ppm of seven different analytes. c) 2D feature representation 0.5-mm width and GQDs-coated microfluidic gas sensors exposed to100 ppm of seven different analytes (300 ℃ at the sensing layer of the MOS sensor).
The selectivity of the unmodified microfluidic gas sensor.
The selectivity of the 0.5 mm width microfluidic gas sensor.
The selectivity of the microfluidic gas sensor coated with Parylene C and GQDs.
The selectivity of the microfluidic gas sensor with 0.5 mm width and GQDs coating.

head.ce:title: Enhanced selectivity of microfluidic gas sensors by modifying microchannel geometry and surface chemistry with graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Engineering, University of British Columbia, Kelowna, BC, Canada
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Engineering, University of British Columbia, Kelowna, BC, Canada

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: EME4242 - 1137 Alumni Ave, Kelowna, BC, V1V 1V7, Canada.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Among the gas sensing technologies, microfluidic gas sensors have garnered attention because of their sensitivity, compact size, and low cost. In this study, we demonstrate improved selectivity of microfluidic gas sensors toward volatile organic compounds by increasing the effect of adsorption of analytes on the surface of the sensor’s microchannel through increasing the ratio of the surface area (in contact with analyte) to the volume of the microchannel. First, the effect of microchannel geometry modification (reduction of width) is studied through a computational parametric approach (which is validated experimentally). The results show an average improvement of 93.44 % and 60.1 % in selectivity toward polar and nonpolar VOCs, respectively. In the next step, the surface of the microchannel is modified with graphene quantum dots, which has a two-fold effect on VOCs adsorption: (i) increasing the surface area, and (ii) adding functional groups. The experimental results of this step show an average improvement of 101.45 % and 98.82 % in the sensor’s selectivity for the smallest widths toward polar and nonpolar VOCs, respectively. These results indicate that increasing the ratio of surface area (in contact with analyte) to the volume of the microchannel and adding functionalized nanofeatures to the microchannel surface area are promising ways to enhance the selectivity of microfluidic gas sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Monitoring volatile organic compounds (VOCs) released from different sources (e.g., food processing and healthcare industries [1–3]) is of great importance due to their impacts on health, environment, and safety [4,5]. In addition to the need for high sensitivity, one of the challenges in VOC monitoring is specificity (selectivity), i.e., accurate detection of a specific gas (for instance, detection of VOCs in indoor environments in which different analytes exist [6]). The main methods for the detection of VOCs include gas chromatography [7,8], mass spectrometry [9], colorimetric gas sensing [10], and electronic noses [11,12]. However, these devices are bulky and expensive, require expert operators, may suffer from low sensitivity and/or selectivity towards target analytes in the mixture, and experience significant sensor drift [13–16]. Considering these limitations of currently available gas sensing technologies, microfluidic gas sensor platforms have gained more attention during the past few years as a VOC monitoring technology as they are compact, low cost, user-friendly, and highly sensitive with low power consumption [17–22]. Such platforms usually consist of a detector (e.g., a metal oxide semiconductor (MOS) sensor [17]), a microchannel, and an electrical circuit. Each test with these sensors includes exposure and recovery courses during which the sensor is exposed to target analyte and fresh air, respectively. Similar to other gas sensing methods, the performance of microfluidic gas sensors can be measured in the terms of sensor response, selectivity, and response time. These metrics vary mainly based on the combination of three phenomena, namely mass diffusion and surface adsorption/desorption occurring inside the microchannel, and reactions at the surface of the sensing element of the detector [19,20]. In essence, after entering the microchannel, analytes reach the sensing element at different times based on their diffusion kinetics and surface adsorption/desorption rates inside the microchannel [23]. As a result, a unique response signal is obtained for each analyte or mixture of analytes. The greater the difference in arrival time between analytes at the sensing element, the greater the difference between their response signals (selectivity). As a result, to improve the selectivity of microfluidic gas sensors, the effects of mass diffusion and/or surface adsorption/desorption must be tuned. However, the mass diffusion cannot be controlled readily as it depends on both the analyte and the environmental conditions. On the other hand, the effect of surface adsorption/desorption, and hence selectivity, can solely be enhanced by increasing the microchannel surface area to volume ratio (MCs/MCv).

In the present work, we investigate three methods for increasing the surface areas to volume ratio (MCs/MCv) of microfluidic gas sensors and report their effects on sensor selectivity. These methods include reduction of the microchannel width (width=0.5 mm, 1 mm, 1.5 mm, 2 mm, 2.5 mm, and 3 mm), the introduction of nanofeatures and functional groups on the microchannel walls, and the combination of both modifications. The effect of decreasing the width of the microchannel is first conducted computationally using 3D simulation modeling which is validated using experimental results. Decision-making graphs are provided based on computational model results to help the decision-makers select proper widths based on their application’s needs. The effect of adding nanofeatures and functional groups on the performance of the microfluidic gas sensors is then experimentally studied. Due to the unique structural and mechanical properties, GQDs were used to create nanofeatures on the microchannel's walls. This zero-dimensional carbon material with carboxyl and the hydroxyl groups can potentially increase the adsorption rate of VOCs on the microchannel’s walls and consequently improve the selectivity of the sensor. The effect of the combination of the modifications is finally studied experimentally using a GQDs-coated 0.5-mm width microfluidic gas sensor. To determine selectivity, the modified sensors were exposed to 100 ppm of seven different analytes (methanol, ethanol, propanol, pentanol, hexane, hexanal, and toluene) and their responses to analytes are recorded, analyzed, and compared to the bare sensor. All methods show significant improvements in the selectivity of the sensor and hence provide a proof-of-concept for future selectivity enhancement based on increasing the effect of surface adsorption/desorption in comparison with mass diffusion in the microfluidic gas sensors.

A 3D simulated model of a microfluidic gas sensor was developed using COMSOL Multiphysics 5.5. The model is based on three main phenomena occurring inside a microfluidic gas sensor: (1) mass diffusion, (2) surface adsorption/desorption, and (3) reactions occurring at the sensing layer of the detector [20]. In each computational test, the sensor is initially exposed to an analyte (methanol, ethanol, propanol, pentanol, hexane, hexanal, and toluene), and then it is recovered in the fresh air.

The influence of the width of the microchannel on the response of the sensor was studied by a computational parametric study (Table S1). Sensors with different widths (0.5 mm, 1 mm, 1.5 mm, 2 mm, 2.5 mm, and 3 mm) were exposed to seven analytes and the response signals of the sensor were recorded.

The schematic of the simulation model is shown in Fig. 1 a). The model consists of three parts: the microchannel inlet/outlet, the microchannel, and the MOS sensor housing. The dimensions of each part are provided in Table S2. The initial concentration of the analytes inside the microchannel is set to zero and the microchannel is filled with air at a velocity of zero. The temperature and pressure are similar to room conditions. No flux boundary condition is applied to all of the walls. For each test, during the exposure, the inlet of the sensor is exposed to 100 ppm of the analyte.

Mass diffusion occurs during both exposure and recovery courses; analytes diffuse through the microchannel at different rates based on their respective diffusion coefficients in air. The diffusion coefficients of different analytes used in this study are gathered from the literature [24,25] and the Wilke-Lee estimation model [26]. To add mass diffusion to the study, the Maxwell-Stefan multicomponent mass transfer equation is used (Eq. (1)),
(1)
∇ x a = -
∑
β = 1
N
1
C D
α β
( x β N α - x α N β ) ; ; , ; α = 1,2 , 3 , . . . , N
in which x α , N α , C , and D
α β
are mole fraction, flux, total concentration, and the diffusion coefficient of analyte α in the component β , respectively.

During the exposure and recovery processes, the molecules of the analytes adsorb/desorb on the walls of the microchannel. To add this phenomenon to the model, the adsorption isotherm model is used in which the net rate of the adsorption/desorption is dependent on the vacant sites on the walls, the concentration of the analyte, and adsorption/desorption constants. This rate can be shown as Eq. (2) [27]:
(2)
R
a d
= k A C A C S - k d C
A . S
where R
a d
is the net rate of adsorption, K a and K d are adsorption and desorption constants, respectively, and C A , C S , and C
A . S
represent the concentration of the analyte, the vacant sites, and the occupied sites on the walls, respectively.

Exposure to different analytes at the same concentration with the same MOS sensor will result in different signals because of the MOS sensor reactions. In the case of using an n-type MOS sensor, the reaction between the analyte and the oxygen on the surface of the sensing layer changes the conductivity of the sensor by releasing electrons, as shown in Eq. (3) [28,29].
(3)
C H 3 O H + 2 O - → C O 2 + 2 H 2 O + 2 e -

To add the MOS sensor reactions in the simulation study, the sensitivity characteristics curves R s / R 0 as a function of the gas concentration (in ppm) are used and considered in the model ( R s is the sensor resistance during exposure to an analyte and R 0 is the sensor resistance in fresh air provided by the manufacturer of the MOS sensor (here we used sensors from Figaro Inc.)). These curves are calculated at standard test conditions as defined by the manufacturer (i.e., air at 20 ± 2℃, 65 ± 5% RH, circuit and heater voltage of 5.0 ± 0.05 DC, and 7 days conditioning period before the test).

The microfluidic gas sensor used in this study (Fig. 1 b)) is comprised of three parts: microchannel, MOS detector, and electrical circuit. The microchannel is 3D printed with VeroClear RGD810 [18,19] using a Connex 500 3D printer and it is coated with a 5-μm Parylene C layer (poly (p-xylylene) polymer, CAS No: 28,804-46-8) to provide a chemically-inert barrier layer using a chemical vapor deposition (CVD) Parylene deposition system (SCS Labcoater® PDS 2010). The dimensions of the microchannel are provided in Table S2. The MOS sensor, located at the end of the microchannel, is a Figaro 2602 sensor which is connected to the electrical circuit (Figure S1) and a 5-volt DC power source (VC), the working temperature of this MOS sensor in this study is 300 ℃ which is maintained using a microheater connected to a 5-volt DC power source (VH (Figure S1)). To study the effect of nanofeatures and functional groups a microfluidic gas sensor was prepared by modifying a Parylene C-coated microchannel by adding GQDs. GQDs were synthesized by pyrolysis of citric acid [30]. The microchannels surface was modified with GQDs by deep coating the oxygen-plasma-treated Parylene C-coated microchannels in the GQDs solution in dark for 12 h followed by incubating the modified microchannels to 70℃ for 1 h. The synthesized GQDs were characterized using fluorescence spectroscopy and Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. Additionally, the modified channels were characterized using water contact angle measurements and scanning electron microscopy (SEM).

The experimental sampling setup used for this study includes a 40-liter chamber with an inlet/outlet, a heater, a fan, an analyte injection valve, a temperature sensor (all tests were conducted at room temperature), a humidity sensor (all tests were conducted at room humidity), and a linear actuator which controls exposure and recovery of the sensors (multiple sensors can be exposed and recovered for each test) (Fig. 1 c)). By connecting the inlet to a compressor and opening the outlet, the chamber is flushed for 5 min before each test to ensure that the testing chamber contains only air. The target analyte is injected through the analyte injection valve (position directly on top of the heater surface) using a pipette. The volume of the target analyte is calculated based on its density, molecular weight, and the ideal gas law [19]. The heater and the fan increase the rate of evaporation and dispersion of the target analyte within the chamber to ensure uniform distribution (15 min waiting time between the analyte injection step and the sensor exposure step). After the analyte is completely evaporated and homogenously dispersed within the testing chamber, the microfluidic sensors are exposed to the target analyte by extending the linear actuator. The sensors are exposed to the analyte simultaneously to cancel any unwanted effects such as changes in the concentration of the analyte inside the testing chamber. Signals are recorded using the electronic circuit provided by the manufacturer and a raspberry pi microcontroller.

The data collection for each experimental test consists of three main steps. First, signals of the sensors were recorded in the absence of the analyte for 10 s to check the baselines (to ensure they were completely recovered from the previous test). Next, the sensors were exposed to the target analyte. Finally, the sensors were exposed to air for the recovery step. To ensure the results are reproducible, each experiment was repeated three times.

The performance of the microfluidic gas sensors can be measured using parameters such as sensor response, selectivity, and response time. Sensor response is defined as sensor resistance when it is exposed to the air ( R a ) compared to the resistance as exposed to the target analyte ( R g ) [19,31]
(4)
S =
R a
R g

The selectivity of a gas sensor shows its ability to separate the response signals from different analytes. To visualize and qualitatively investigate the selectivity of the sensors, a feature extraction method is used in this study. Based on previous studies [17], two features are extracted from each sensor response: 1) the time when the response reaches 50 % of its maximum value, and 2) the time when the response reaches 95 % of its maximum value. These features can be used to demonstrate the selectivity visually in a 2D feature space. To compare the selectivity of different sensors among different analytes quantitively, the 2D Euclidean distance of each feature was calculated for each sensor between every pair of analytes and was considered as a selectivity indicator [17] (Eq. (5)).
(5)
S e l =
( S
1 i
- S
1 j
) 2 + ( S
2 i
- S
2 j
) 2

In the previous equation, S e l is the selectivity indicator, S
1 i
and S
1 j
are feature 1 values for target analytes i and j , respectively, and S
2 i
and S
2 j
are feature 2 values for target analytes i and j , respectively.
In this study, the response time is defined as the time it takes the sensor signal to reach 90 % of its maximum value [17,32,33] as it is exposed to the target analyte.

To validate the simulation model, we compared the computational and experimental responses. The response of the sensors coated with Parylene C exposed to 100 ppm of methanol and ethanol are selected for this comparison. As shown in Fig. 2 a) and b), the computational and experimental results agree with each other (average RMSE = 0.054), indicating the efficacy of the numerical model.

To find the optimal number of computational cells while maintaining low computational time, six case studies were investigated numerically. The response of the sensor exposed to 100 ppm of ethanol is depicted versus the number of the cells (changing from 10,000 cells to 2,000,000 cells) in Fig. 2 c). By increasing the number of the cells, the sensor response converges to a constant value of 1.560, and as a result, the number of the cells is set as 80,000.

Figure S2 shows the concentration profiles inside the microchannel when the sensor is exposed to 100 ppm of ethanol at different time steps (0, 50, 100, 150, 200, and 240 s), to visually understand the process happening inside the microchannel. Initially, the sensor only contains air; after exposure to ethanol, the analyte diffuses through the channel. After passing through the channel ethanol reaches the sensor and its concentration increases until the end of the exposure phase. After the exposure course, the recovery phase begins in which the ethanol diffuses out through the channel, and the concentration of the analyte in the channel drops.

To have reference values for further comparisons between modified and unmodified microfluidic gas sensors, a simple unmodified microfluidic gas sensor was fabricated. This sensor was exposed to methanol, ethanol, propanol, pentanol, hexane, hexanal, and toluene using the experiment setup. The responses of the sensor were recorded. The resistances of the sensor as a function of time are shown in Fig. 3 a), and sensor responses and response times are shown in Fig. 3 b) and Figure S3 a). As can be seen during exposure, sensor resistance drops (the average response time of sensor was found to be 117 s (Fig. 3 b)), and during the recovery phase, the resistance value increases to finally reach the initial value. To visualize the ability of the sensor in distinguishing different analytes and to calculate the selectivity indicator (Eq. (5)) two features were selected from sensor response. 2D representation of features is shown in Fig. 3 c), and the selectivity indicators are provided in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of decreasing width of the microchannel
ce:section.ce:para: Decreasing the width of the microchannel will increase the likelihood of surface adsorption/desorption effect by increasing the ratio of MCs/MCv. The effect of the width of the microchannel on the response of the sensor was studied computationally by exposing the sensors with different widths (0.5 mm, 1 mm, 1.5 mm, 2 mm, 2.5 mm, and 3 mm) to methanol, ethanol, propanol, pentanol, hexane, hexanal, and toluene.

Resistances and response signals of the sensor with 0.5 mm width are shown in Fig. 4 a), and Figure S3 b). Based on the exposure regions it can be seen that the sensor with the narrower channel is more capable of distinguishing different analytes response signals. On the other hand, decreasing the width of the microchannel reduces the number of gas molecules that can diffuse through the microchannel to reach the sensing layer of the MOS sensor during the exposure time. Therefore, the response of the sensors drops when the width of the microchannel is decreased. To investigate the effect of the channel width reduction on the response, response time, and selectivity of the sensor quantitatively, Eqs. (4), and (5) are used to calculate the performance measures. The results are shown in Fig. 4 b), c), and Table 2 .

By looking at Fig. 4 b) and c), and comparing Table 1 with Table 2, it can be understood that reducing the width of the microchannel from 3 mm to 0.5 mm improves the selectivity of the sensor by an average of 93.44 % toward polar VOCs and 60.1 % toward nonpolar VOCs while the response of the sensor drops (by an average of 39 %) and the response time increases (by an average of 27 %). Reducing the width of the microchannel decreases the number of vacant sites on the microchannel’s walls, but the ratio of the wall surface area to the microchannel volume is increased which increases the effect of the surface adsorption/desorption (in comparison to mass diffusion) and improves the selectivity of the sensor. The more dominant the adsorption/desorption effect (in comparison to the mass diffusion), the slower the rate of the molecules transfer to the sensing layer and hence the greater the response time. For many applications high selectivity is required and therefore the response time increment can be neglected. The lower sensor response resulting from decreasing the microchannel width can be addressed by using a more sensitive detector at the end of the channel.

The width of the microchannel influences the selectivity, sensor response, and response time of the sensor, therefore decision-makers have to choose the optimal width based on the parameters of sensor response, selectivity, and response time in their applications. In Fig. 5 , two graphs are provided for the decision-makers to help choose the best geometry (width) based on the trade-offs between selectivity, sensor response, and response time for their application. In these graphs, the average sensor selectivity is plotted as a function of the average sensor response and the average response time for the four analytes.

The optical properties of GQDs were studied by fluorescence spectroscopy (200–800 nm) (Fig. 6 a)). GQDs were excited with a 365 nm UV beam and emitted blue light (460 nm), indicating that GQDs were successfully synthesized [30]. The GQDs were also characterized by FTIR (Fig. 6 b)). GQDs exhibited absorption peaks at the carboxyl group and the hydroxyl group at 1396 cm−1 (for COH stretching), 1077 cm−1 (for CO stretching), and 1564 cm−1 (for CC stretching). The polar VOCs (such as methanol, ethanol, propanol, pentanol) prefer to interact with polar functional groups on the surface of GQDs. Nonpolar VOCs (such as hexane, hexanal, and toluene) interact with hydrophilic sites on the surface of the channel. Coating the microchannel with GQDs also changes the surface area (by adding microfeatures and nanostructures) which increases the chance of interactions between VOCs and microchannels wall. As a result, modification of the surface of the microchannel by GQDs which introduces carboxyl and the hydroxyl groups to the walls and increases the surface area of the microchannel increases the adsorption of VOCs.

The functional groups of the GQDs introduce an electrostatic charge to the microchannel that improves the hydrophilicity of the surface as confirmed by water contact angle measurements (Fig. 6 c) and d)). The non-coated and GQDs-modified microchannels showed a water contact angle of 55.1° and 29.3°, respectively.

The SEM images confirmed the successful modification of the microchannel with the Parylene C and GQDs. As shown in Fig. 6 e), f), and g), GQDs cause an increase in the roughness of the channel surface which consequently increases the available binding sites of the channel to interact with analytes.

To investigate the performance of the fabricated sensor, a modified microfluidic gas sensor (coated with GQDs) was exposed to seven different analytes followed by exposure to the air. Averaged resistances and sensor responses are provided in Fig. 7 a), and Figure S3 c). Response values and response times are provided in Fig. 7 b). By coating the microchannel with GQDs, the sensor’s capability of distinguishing between the target analytes is improved, as illustrated by the clear distinction between features using a 2D feature plot in Fig. 7 c).

To quantitatively evaluate the changes in the selectivity of the sensor, the average response of the sensor to each analyte was used to calculate the sensor selectivity indicator using Eq.s (5). selectivity indicators are provided in Table 3 .

As shown in Fig. 7 c) and Table 3, coating the microchannel with GQDs improves the selectivity of the sensor by an average of 98.72 % for polar and 86.6 % for nonpolar VOCs, respectively. This significant enhancement, however, reduces the sensor response (by an average of 30.6 %) and increases the response time of sensors (by an average of 10.25 %). However, these negative side effects can be considered negligible in many applications where high selectivity is required. In addition to introducing carboxyl and the hydroxyl groups to the wall of the channel, coating the microchannel with GQDs increases the surface area that the analytes are in contact with. The greater the surface area the greater the number of vacant sites ( C s ) on the microchannel’s walls which in turn increases the surface adsorption/desorption effect. Therefore, for the sensor coated with GQDs, the surface adsorption/desorption rate is dominant in comparison to the unmodified sensor leading to an improved selectivity. The higher surface adsorption/desorption rate, however, makes the molecules reach the sensing layer slower and causes an increase in the response time of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of combination of both modifications
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the synergic effect of reducing the microchannel’s width and GQDs coating, a 0.5 mm-width GQDs-coated microchannel was fabricated and exposed to methanol, ethanol, propanol, pentanol, hexane, hexanal, and toluene. Sensor resistances, and responses of the sensor are depicted in Fig. 8 a), and Figure S3 d). By both reducing the width of the microchannel and coating with GQDs, the influence of the surface adsorption/desorption (compared to the mass diffusion) on the sensor response increases. This makes the sensor, more capable of differentiating different analytes. As it can be seen from Fig. 8 c) and Table 4 , the combination of these modifications can improve the selectivity of the sensor by the average of 101.45 % and 98.82 % for polar and nonpolar VOCs, respectively, which are more than the improvements resulted from each modification solely. A better selectivity of the sensor toward polar VOCs can be attributed to enhanced interactions with the functional groups on the surface of the 0.5 mm-width microchannel coated with GQDs.

This improvement in the selectivity of the sensor, however, brings negative effects on sensor response and response time (Fig. 8 b)) due to increased surface adsorption/desorption. The response drops by an average of 46.19 % and the response time increases by an average of 24.57 %. As a result, the sensor response/response time of the 0.5 mm-width GQDs-coated microfluidic sensor is less/more than the 0.5 mm only Parylene C coated, and 3 mm GQDs-coated sensors.

In this study, the selectivity of microfluidic gas sensors toward VOCs is improved by changing the surface chemistry and geometry of the microchannels. In the first part of this study, a parametric study is conducted on the geometry of the microchannel using computational tools and experimental validation with seven analytes (methanol, ethanol, propanol, pentanol, hexane, hexanal, and toluene). Decreasing the width of the microchannel to increase the surface area to the volume ratio of the microchannel (MCs/MCv) resulted in increased selectivity of the sensor by an average of 93.44 % to polar VOCs, and 60.1 % to nonpolar VOCs as the surface adsorption/desorption effect became dominant over mass diffusion. Second, the microchannels were coated with GQDs to increase the MCs/MCv ratio and to introduce carboxyl and the hydroxyl groups to the microchannel surfaces. The performance was compared to those coated with only Parylene C. Adding hydrophilic nanostructures on the microchannel’s walls improved the selectivity of the sensor to polar VOCs by an average of 98.72 % and to nonpolar VOCs by an average of 86.6 %. Finally, the effect of the combination of the two modifications was studied. The combination of decreasing the width of the microchannel and coating the surface area with GQDs can significantly increase the MCs/MCv, resulting in a 101.45 % improvement (selectivity) for polar VOCs and 98.82 % improvement for nonpolar Occupied to the bare sensor. In conclusion, increasing the MCs/MCv ratio through alterations in surface chemistry and width of microchannels is shown to be a promising strategy to improve the selectivity of microfluidic gas sensors. These improved gas sensors can then be used in applications where high selectivity is required.
This work was supported by MITACS, Inc./Accelerate with FortisBC Energy Inc. Grant number F19-05968 and NSERC DGDND-2018-05726.

Mahan Ghazi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Visualization, Project administration. Sajjad Janfaza: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Hamed Tahmooressi: Validation, Resources, Data curation. Adithya Ravishankara: Resources, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Emily Earl: Resources, Data curation, Writing - review & editing. Nishat Tasnim: Writing - review & editing. Mina Hoorfar: Conceptualization, Writing - review & editing, Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to express our very great appreciation to Dr. Mohammad Arjmand and Dr. Ali Akbari Sehat for their help during this research work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.130050.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mahan Ghazi received his B.A.Sc. Degree in Mechanical Engineering from Iran University of Science and Technology (IUST) in 2019. He joined the Advanced Thermo-Fluidic Laboratory (ATFL) at the university of British Columbia as a M.A.Sc. student in 2019. His research is focused on microfluidic based gas sensing methods, computational fluid mechanics, and data analysis.

Sajjad Janfaza received his Ph.D. in nanobiotechnology in 2019 from Tarbiat Modares University. He recently joined the Advanced Thermo-Fluidic Laboratory (ATFL) at the University of British Columbia (UBC) as a postdoctoral research fellow. His research interest is the integration of nanotechnology and microfabrication techniques for advanced biomedical applications.

Hamed Tahmooressi is a PhD student in Mechanical Engineering, at the University of British Columbia. With a background in computational physics, his current research is on gas sensors using nano/microstructures.

Adithya Ravishankara received his BSc. in Physics from the University of Alberta. He joined the Advanced Thermofluidic Laboratory (ATFL) in 2018 and has since worked on numerous projects based on the development of sensing platforms. He is currently working on a Master of Applied Science at the University of British Columbia developing sensors for the measurement of cannabinoids.

Emily Earl received her B.A.Sc. Degree in Mechanical Engineering in 2019 from the University of British Columbia (UBC) and has since then been working towards a M.A.Sc at the Advanced Thermo-Fluidic Laboratory (ATFL) at UBC. Her current research is focused on adapting hydrogels to be better suited for particular biomedical applications in the gastrointestinal field.

Nishat Tasnim is the research lab manager in the Advanced Thermo-Fluidic Laboratory (ATFL) at the University of British Columbia (UBC). She studied microbiology and immunology at the University of British Columbia and obtained a M.Sc. in biochemistry and molecular biology, specializing in gut microbiome development and bioinformatics analysis. At ATFL her primary responsibilities are to support high quality research and oversee the development of microfluidic lab-on-chip devices and gas/liquid sensor prototypes with applications in nuisance sewer gas detection, natural gas leakage, biomarker identification, and breath-analysis.

Dr. Mina Hoorfar is a Professor and Director of the School of Engineering, The University of British Columbia, Canada. She is known nationally and internationally for her research and innovation in microfluidics and its applications to device miniaturization and sensing technologies. Her group has extensive experience in surface science and the development of lab-on-chip devices for cancer cell detection, online monitoring of water quality, and portable sensors for sensitive and selective detection of target gases (such as biomarkers in breath, nuisance sewer gases and GHGs). Her work has led to several patents, licenses, and commercialization with industrial partners in different sectors including oil/gas, wastewater, health, and safety and security.
